walkthrough.title: Adjust the Deck for Player Count
walkthrough.text: Let's set up Point Salad. First, you need to adjust the deck based on how many players you have. Use six of each vegetable type per player. So with three players, you use eighteen of each vegetable, which is fifty-four cards total. With four players, use all 108 cards. Remove any extras and put them back in the box without looking.

walkthrough.title: Shuffle and Deal Three Piles
walkthrough.text: Take all the remaining cards and shuffle them with the scoring side face up. Now deal them into three roughly equal face-down piles. These are your three draw piles. The top card of each pile shows a scoring condition, and that is visible to everyone.

walkthrough.title: Create the Vegetable Market
walkthrough.text: Flip the top two cards from each draw pile face up and place them below their pile, showing the vegetable side. You should now have a market of six face-up vegetable cards arranged in a three-by-two grid. This is the market where players will draft vegetables.

walkthrough.title: Choose the First Player
walkthrough.text: Whoever most recently ate a salad goes first. If nobody can remember, just pick someone at random. Play goes clockwise from there. That is it for setup. Point Salad is one of the fastest games to get on the table.

summary.title: Adjust the Deck for Player Count
bullets: 108 double-sided cards: 6 veggie types, 18 each
Use 6 of each type per player (e.g., 3 players = 54 cards)
Remove extras to box unseen

summary.title: Shuffle and Deal Three Piles
bullets: Shuffle all cards scoring-side up
Deal into 3 roughly equal face-down draw piles
Top card of each pile shows a scoring condition

summary.title: Create the Vegetable Market
bullets: Flip 2 cards per pile → 6 face-up veggie cards (3×2 grid)
Market sits below the 3 draw piles

summary.title: Choose the First Player
bullets: Most recently ate a salad goes first (or random)
Play proceeds clockwise

walkthrough.title: Game Overview
walkthrough.text: Point Salad is a lightning-fast card drafting game. Every card is double-sided: one side shows a vegetable and the other side shows a scoring condition. On your turn you either draft scoring cards or vegetables, then at the end you tally up your points. The player with the most points wins.

walkthrough.title: Option One: Take a Scoring Card
walkthrough.text: On your turn, you can take one scoring card from the top of any of the three draw piles. These cards show conditions like two points per carrot or five points for each lettuce and tomato pair. Place the card in front of you with the scoring side face up. You only get one scoring card if you choose this option.

walkthrough.title: Option Two: Take Vegetables
walkthrough.text: Alternatively, you can take one or two vegetable cards from the face-up market. You can pick any combination from any columns. Place them in front of you in your veggie collection. After you take vegetables, refill the empty market slots by flipping cards from the draw pile above each empty slot.

walkthrough.title: Flipping Scoring Cards
walkthrough.text: Here is a special action you can do on your turn in addition to your normal draft. You may flip one of your own scoring cards to its vegetable side. This is permanent and you cannot flip it back. This is useful when a scoring card does not fit your strategy, but the vegetable on the back would help your other scoring cards.

walkthrough.title: Empty Pile Rules
walkthrough.text: If one of the three draw piles runs out of cards, take the largest remaining pile and split it roughly in half to create a new pile. If market slots cannot be refilled because there are no cards left in the pile above them, those slots simply stay empty for the rest of the game.

walkthrough.title: Game End and Scoring
walkthrough.text: The game ends when all cards have been drafted from both the draw piles and the market. Now evaluate each of your scoring cards against your vegetable collection. Some cards give points per vegetable type, some reward pairs or sets, some give bonuses for having the most of something, and some even give negative points for certain vegetables. Add up all your scoring card results. The player with the highest total wins. If there is a tie, the player with the most vegetable cards wins.

summary.title: Game Overview
bullets: Fast card-drafting game with double-sided cards
One side = vegetable, other side = scoring condition
Most points at end wins

walkthrough.title: Welcome to Point Salad
walkthrough.text: Welcome to your first game of Point Salad! We will walk through a few rounds with three example players. The market has six face-up vegetables and three draw piles with scoring cards on top. Let's see how drafting works in practice.

walkthrough.title: Player 1's Turn: Grab a Scoring Card
walkthrough.text: Player 1 looks at the three scoring cards on top of the draw piles. One says two points per carrot, another says three points per pepper and tomato pair, and the third says five points if you have the most lettuce. Player 1 grabs the two points per carrot card. Now they know what vegetables to chase.

walkthrough.title: Why Scoring First Can Be Smart
walkthrough.text: Taking a scoring card early gives you a clear direction. Player 1 now knows they want carrots. Every future turn, they can evaluate the market through that lens. Without a scoring card, you are drafting vegetables blindly and hoping they match something later.

walkthrough.title: Player 2's Turn: Take Two Vegetables
walkthrough.text: Player 2 decides to grab two vegetable cards from the market. They take a carrot and an onion. The empty market slots get refilled from the draw piles above them. Player 2 does not have a scoring card yet, but they are building a veggie collection to match future scoring picks.

walkthrough.title: Player 3's Turn: The Mistake
walkthrough.text: Player 3 takes a scoring card that says negative two points for each cabbage. Then on the next turn, Player 3 takes two cabbages from the market because they are the only vegetables available. Now those cabbages are actively hurting their score.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Watch for Negative Scoring
walkthrough.text: Some scoring cards penalize you for having certain vegetables. Before you take a scoring card, check whether its penalties conflict with vegetables you already have or might be forced to take. If you already grabbed a card that penalizes cabbages, avoid cabbages at all costs. And remember, you can always flip that scoring card to its vegetable side if it is hurting you.

walkthrough.title: Player 1 Drafts Carrots
walkthrough.text: Player 1 spots two carrots in the market and grabs them both. With their two points per carrot scoring card, each carrot is now worth two points. Two carrots means four points from just one scoring card. Player 1 is building a focused strategy.

walkthrough.title: Player 3's Second Mistake
walkthrough.text: Player 3 takes another scoring card that says three points per onion and tomato pair. But they also still have the negative two per cabbage card. Player 3 is now spread across too many different scoring conditions and has cabbages they cannot get rid of. Their collection is pulling in opposite directions.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Build Synergy, Not Variety
walkthrough.text: The best Point Salad strategy is to pick scoring cards that reward the same vegetables. If your first card rewards carrots, look for more carrot-related scoring. Taking five different scoring cards that each want different vegetables means you will never collect enough of any one type. Focus on two or three vegetable types and stack scoring cards that reward them.

walkthrough.title: Player 3's Recovery: Flip the Bad Card
walkthrough.text: Player 3 realizes the negative cabbage card is dragging them down. On their next turn, they flip it to its vegetable side, turning it into a pepper. Now instead of losing points for cabbages, they have a pepper that might help their onion and tomato scoring. Smart recovery.

walkthrough.title: The Final Rounds
walkthrough.text: As the game winds down, the draw piles shrink and market choices become limited. Player 1 has been stacking carrot-related scoring and has six carrots. Player 2 has a balanced mix. Player 3 recovered from early mistakes and focused on onion and tomato pairs.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: Congratulations! You have played through your first game of Point Salad. You saw how to draft scoring cards and vegetables, learned to watch out for negative scoring conditions, and discovered the power of building synergy between your scoring cards. Remember: flip bad cards, focus on two or three veggies, and stack scoring that rewards the same collection.

walkthrough.title: Before You Toss Your Next Salad
walkthrough.text:
Before you play a full game, here are three things that can help.

First, check out General Tips for drafting strategy and reading the market.

Second, reference the Appendix for a quick guide to scoring card types and vegetable counts.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

walkthrough.title: Score Cards First, Veggies Second
walkthrough.text: In your first few turns, lean toward taking scoring cards from the draw piles. Scoring cards give you a roadmap for what vegetables to collect. Without scoring cards, you are just picking up random vegetables and hoping they match something later. Having two or three scoring cards early lets you make informed decisions for the rest of the game.

walkthrough.title: Focus on Two or Three Vegetable Types
walkthrough.text: There are six vegetable types in Point Salad, but trying to collect all of them spreads you too thin. Pick two or three types that your scoring cards reward and chase those aggressively. A player with eight carrots and a two points per carrot card earns sixteen points from one card alone. That kind of focus beats having a little of everything.

walkthrough.title: Stack Synergistic Scoring Cards
walkthrough.text: The real power in Point Salad comes from multiple scoring cards that reward the same vegetables. If you have two points per carrot and three points for each carrot and onion pair, every carrot you draft is working double duty. Look for scoring cards that compound each other rather than pulling in different directions.

walkthrough.title: Flip Bad Scoring Cards
walkthrough.text: Do not let a bad scoring card drag you down. If you took a card that gives negative points for onions and you end up with several onions, flip that card to its vegetable side. You trade a liability for a useful vegetable. Many new players forget they can do this, but it is one of the most powerful moves in the game.

walkthrough.title: Watch What Opponents Draft
walkthrough.text: Pay attention to which vegetables your opponents are collecting. If someone is hoarding tomatoes, do not take a most tomatoes scoring card because you will probably lose that race. Instead, find a vegetable type that nobody else is competing for. Uncontested vegetables are free points.

walkthrough.title: Deny Draft When It Makes Sense
walkthrough.text: Sometimes the best play is to take a vegetable your opponent desperately needs, even if it only slightly helps you. If your neighbor has a five points for most peppers card and there is one pepper in the market, snagging it can cost them five points while costing you nothing. Deny drafting is especially powerful in two or three player games where each card matters more.

walkthrough.title: Majority Cards Are High Risk, High Reward
walkthrough.text: Scoring cards that award points for having the most of a vegetable type can be worth ten or more points. But they are also risky because multiple players might compete for the same majority. Only take a majority card if you already have a lead in that vegetable or if nobody else seems interested. A contested majority wastes draft picks for everyone involved.

summary.title: Score Cards First, Veggies Second
bullets: Prioritize scoring cards in early turns for direction
2-3 scoring cards before heavy veggie drafting
Scoring cards = roadmap for the rest of the game

walkthrough.title: Reading the Market State
walkthrough.text: Advanced players pay close attention to which vegetables appear in the market and which scoring cards are visible on top of the draw piles. If the market is heavy on tomatoes and a scoring card rewards tomatoes, grab that scoring card before anyone else notices. The market state tells you what is available right now and hints at what might come next.

walkthrough.title: Timing Your Scoring Card Picks
walkthrough.text: Early scoring cards give direction, but late scoring cards can be even more powerful. By the midgame, you know exactly what vegetables you have collected. Taking a scoring card that perfectly matches your existing collection is almost guaranteed points. The trick is to balance early direction with late-game precision.

walkthrough.title: Combo Scoring Strategies
walkthrough.text: The highest scores come from combo strategies where multiple scoring cards reward the same vegetables. For example, if you have two points per carrot, three points for each carrot and lettuce pair, and one point for each vegetable type you have at least three of, every carrot advances all three scoring cards simultaneously. Build combos early and draft to feed them.

walkthrough.title: Negative Card Exploitation
walkthrough.text: Some scoring cards have negative conditions that scare away most players. But if you can avoid the penalized vegetable, the positive part of that card can be very strong. Cards like five points per tomato pair minus two per cabbage are excellent if you simply never take a cabbage. Use your opponents' fear of negative cards to grab high-value scoring opportunities they passed on.

walkthrough.title: Two-Card Turns Are Efficient
walkthrough.text: When you take two vegetables from the market, you get twice as many cards as a player who takes one scoring card. This means the veggie-drafting option is more efficient in terms of card count. In the late game, when you already have your scoring direction set, switch to grabbing two vegetables per turn to maximize your collection before the game ends.

walkthrough.title: Metagame and Player Reads
walkthrough.text: In Point Salad, the meta shifts with each draft. If you notice one player grabbing nothing but peppers, steer your scoring cards toward non-pepper types. If two players are fighting over onions, stay out of that war entirely. The best advanced strategy is adapting in real time to what everyone else is doing and finding the path of least resistance.

walkthrough.title: Endgame Flipping Decisions
walkthrough.text: The flip decision becomes critical at game end. Before final scoring, review each of your scoring cards one more time. A card giving you zero or negative points is better off flipped to its vegetable side, where it might boost another scoring card. Even a card giving you only one point might be worth flipping if the vegetable side would earn you three points through other scoring cards. Do the math before you commit.

bullets: Watch which veggies flood the market — match scoring to availability
Visible scoring cards on draw piles hint at combo opportunities
Market state changes every turn — stay adaptive

entries.definition: One side of a double-sided card showing a point condition. Conditions include per-vegetable bonuses, pair bonuses, majority bonuses, set bonuses, and penalties for specific vegetables.
The other side of a double-sided card showing one of six vegetable types. Vegetables are collected to satisfy scoring card conditions.
One of six vegetable types. Red pepper icon. 18 total in a full deck.
One of six vegetable types. Green lettuce icon. 18 total in a full deck.
One of six vegetable types. Orange carrot icon. 18 total in a full deck.
One of six vegetable types. Purple cabbage icon. 18 total in a full deck.
One of six vegetable types. Yellow onion icon. 18 total in a full deck.
One of six vegetable types. Red tomato icon. 18 total in a full deck.
One of three face-down card piles. The top card is visible with its scoring side face up. Players can draft this top scoring card on their turn.
The six face-up vegetable cards arranged in a 3x2 grid below the draw piles. Players can take 1 or 2 vegetables from the market on their turn.
On your turn, you may permanently flip one of your own scoring cards to its vegetable side. This is useful when a scoring condition hurts your score but the vegetable on the back would help.
A scoring condition that awards a fixed number of points for each vegetable of a specific type you have collected. Example: 2 points per Carrot.
A scoring condition that awards points for each pair of two specific vegetable types. Example: 5 points per Lettuce/Tomato pair. Requires one of each type per pair.
A scoring condition that awards a large point bonus to the player with the most of a specific vegetable type. Highly contested and risky if multiple players compete.
A scoring condition that subtracts points for each vegetable of a specific type. Example: -2 per Cabbage. Can be avoided by not drafting that vegetable, or the card can be flipped.
